text: Robotic low anterior resection for a distal sigmoid colon
A fifty-two year old male presents with three months of abdominal pain and hematochesia.
Patient has no family history of GI cancer.
A physical exam revealed internal hemorrhoids and a BMI of twenty-eight.
Colonoscopy showed a friable circumferential mass twenty centimeters from the anal verge.
The colonoscope is visibly obstructed by a circumferential mass in the distal sigmoid colon, preventing further advancement.
The surgeon takes biopsies from the friable circumferential mass located twenty centimeters from the anal verge and applies a tattoo to mark the mass.
Biopsy results showed that there was a microscopic focus of high-grade dysplasia or intramucosal adenocarcinoma.
A CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed, which were negative for distant metastases.
MRI of the abdomen and pelvis showed a seven-centimeter segment of distal sigmoid colon, fifteen centimeters above the anal verge with diffuse thickening of the submucosal and muscularis margins.

Key points include team personal protective precautions, an N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N
The surgeon turns off the insufflator and uses a smoke evacuation device to empty the pneumoperitoneum before making the extraction incision.
The surgeon avoids trans-anal specimen extraction to prevent the release of pneumoperitoneum during the procedure.
At our institution, we use a Buffalo filter plume port active, which allows for pneumoperitoneum and smoke evacuation through ultrafiltration.
A full control dial on the plume port active allows us to control the rate of suction. This whole system is connected to regular suction in the room.
Advantages of robotic surgery during the COVID era include lower levels of personal protective equipment needed for the console surgeon.
The surgical team maintains adequate visualization of the intra-abdominal cavity with reduced pneumoperitoneum pressure set to eight millimeters of mercury.
There is also an ability to minimize electrocautery power by avoiding the use of ultrasonic sealing devices and by easily using clips and sutures.
The surgical setup includes four ports visible on the abdomen: a 12-millimeter port in the right lower quadrant and three additional ports measuring 8.5 millimeters each.
The robotic arms are positioned in the left lower quadrant, targeting the sigmoid colon. Adhesions around the sigmoid colon are carefully lysed.
The surgeon visualizes the sigmoid colon cancer, which is located very low in the pelvis, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon sharply lyses interloop adhesions proximal to the tumor, as well as adhesions between the mesentery and the sigmoid colon, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon addresses the proximal aspect of the tumor, which is adherent to the lateral pelvic wall, using a medial-to-lateral dissection approach.
A cardiac retractor is used to apply anterior traction on the sigmoid colon, exposing the peritoneum over the sacral promontory to create an aortic mesenteric window.
The surgeon uses an infra-inferior mesenteric artery approach to identify and preserve the hypogastric nerves and the left ureter, ensuring these structures are not damaged during the procedure.
A sponge is placed to retract the small bowel, allowing clear visualization of the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery and ligates it one centimeter from its origin using hemoclips, ensuring clear visualization and precise placement of the clips.

Following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, the surgeon continues the dissection of the mesocolon using a medial-to-lateral approach, carefully avoiding injury to the visible inferior mesenteric vein and ureter.
The surgeon traces the inferior mesenteric vein to just below the level of the pancreas, where it is skeletonized, ligated, and divided using hemoclips.

The surgeon enters the omental bursa and continues dissection towards the splenic flexure. The left colon is retracted medially to divide the lateral attachments up to the level of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a partial mesorectal resection, initially mobilizing the rectum posteriorly through sharp dissection between the mesorectal fascia and presacral fascia. The right side of the mesorectum is mobilized first, followed by the left side.

The surgeon connects the circumferential dissection anteriorly from the left side, using the fourth arm to retract the sigmoid colon. This maneuver ensures entry into the correct dissection plane and achieves an adequate distal margin from the tumor.
The surgeon continues the dissection by first moving posteriorly, then proceeding laterally on both the right and left sides, and finally advancing anteriorly to further dissect the distal region.
The surgeon divides the rectum distal to the tumor using multiple 45mm green stapler loads after completing the circumferential dissection.
The robotic arms are repositioned to continue the mobilization of the splenic flexure, with the left colon being retracted to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon sequentially divides the gastrocolic ligament from right to left using multiple firings of the vessel sealer, visibly separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon inspects the mobilized descending and sigmoid colon to ensure it is adequately mobilized to the level of the peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon checks the vascularization of the proposed proximal end using indocyanine green. The stapler port is then extended to eight centimeters.
The surgeon extracts and excises the specimen, places a stapler anvil, and closes the extraction site.
The robotic arms are positioned in the pelvis, and the surgeon performs an anastomosis using a powered EEA stapler.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the anastomosis, visually confirming the absence of any leaks.
Indocyanin green dye is applied to assess the bowel perfusion and visualize the blood supply to the anastomosis site, highlighting areas of vascularization.
Final pathology showed a four centimeter Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the distal sigmoid colon with invasion through the muscularis propria and subsorosal adipose tissue.
The distal margin was three centimeters. Lymph nodes were negative. The patient's post-operative course was uncomplicated and he was discharged on post-operative day two.
On a two-week post-operative visit, he was tolerating his diet and awaiting oncology evaluation for chemotherapy.
